keyLegislation: A New Tax System (Goods and Services Tax) Act 1999 (GST Act)
Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing Act 2006 (AML/CTF Act)
keyConsiderations: Directors owe statutory duties under the Corporations Act including duties of care and diligence (s180), good faith (s181), proper purpose (s184), and to prevent insolvent trading (s588G)
The business judgment rule (s180(2)) provides protection for directors who make informed, good faith decisions — proper documentation of the Bitcoin investment decision is essential
Bitcoin gains are taxed at the company tax rate: 25% for base rate entities (aggregated turnover under $50 million) or 30% for other companies
The 50% CGT discount is NOT available to companies — capital gains are taxed in full at the company rate
Division 7A of the ITAA 1936 applies — any Bitcoin transferred from the company to a shareholder or associate may be deemed a dividend unless proper loan agreements are in place
If the company carries on a business of Bitcoin trading, gains are assessable as ordinary income (not capital gains) under s6-5 ITAA 1997
The company's constitution should be reviewed to ensure it permits investment in digital assets
Related-party transactions involving Bitcoin must be conducted at arm's length and properly documented
ASIC annual reporting requirements apply — financial statements must disclose Bitcoin holdings

Small business CGT concessions (Division 152) may be available if the company has an aggregated turnover under $2 million and the net CGT asset value is under $6 million, but only apply when shares in the company are sold (not when the company sells Bitcoin)
Franking implications — tax paid on Bitcoin gains creates franking credits that can be distributed to shareholders via franked dividends
Private company anti-avoidance provisions (Part IVA) may apply if Bitcoin arrangements are structured primarily to obtain a tax benefit
commonRisks: Director liability if the investment decision is not made with adequate care, diligence, and in good faith
Insolvent trading risk if Bitcoin losses impair the company's ability to pay its debts as and when they fall due
Division 7A deemed dividend if Bitcoin is provided to shareholders or associates without proper documentation
Incorrect tax treatment — classifying trading profits as capital gains or vice versa
Failure to maintain adequate records, leading to ATO penalties and interest
Concentration risk — over-allocating company reserves to a single volatile asset
Loss of Bitcoin through security failures, exchange insolvency, or key management errors
ASIC compliance issues if financial statements do not properly disclose Bitcoin holdings
Related-party transaction breaches if Bitcoin is acquired from or sold to directors or related entities at non-arm's length terms
Cash flow risk — inability to meet operational obligations if Bitcoin value declines significantly
Shareholder disputes if minority shareholders disagree with the investment decision
recommendedProfessionals: Solicitor experienced in corporate law and Corporations Act compliance
Registered Tax Agent with corporate tax and cryptocurrency experience
External Auditor (if the company is required to be audited or elects to be audited)
Accountant familiar with AASB standards for digital asset accounting
Financial Adviser with corporate treasury experience
